claim_summary: Chapter 7 is testimonial evidence assessment with confidence based on directness, page mapping, corroboration, observation limits, OCR quality, and scope.
Witness index contains 18 indexed witnesses/declarants, all classified as inquest testimony, separated by category.
Testimony concentrates around 30 Nov, 1 Dec, June 1949, and later investigative actions such as suitcase recovery and identification circulation.
Chapter distinguishes direct observation, record-based statements, expert opinion, and investigative synthesis.
Table summarizes witness categories and counts.
WIT-0001 reported evening sighting and morning communication; name not legible in OCR; maps to CHR-0006/CHR-0009 and EV-0014/EV-0005.
WIT-0001 supports reported sighting but not settled identification because facial identification is not established.
Strapps and Neill reported second evening observation around 7:20/7:30 p.m., no movement, and concern about condition.
Strapps-Neill testimony corroborates CHR-0007 as observation but not identity of person seen.
John Moss described scene attendance, body position, cigarette, lack of visible violence, transport, and initial property search.
Moss is high value for early observations but exact search and transport sequence require confirmation; missing Tamam Shud slip in initial search is a search-sequence fact.
Leane described identification inquiries, property, suitcase recovery, contents, removed tags, weather, circulation, and exhibit custody.
Leane has high value for source-mapped police actions but OCR distortion and item-level claims require transcription control.
Durham photographed and fingerprinted the deceased, circulated materials, and photographed the paper; original image set and circulation responses incomplete.
Brown compared clothing, noted sand, received Tamam Shud slip, and conducted Rubaiyat inquiries; associated book custody unresolved.
Dwyer described postmortem, external observations, congestion, stomach contents, and medical opinion; OCR medical terminology requires verification.
Cowan received specimens and tested for common poisons without detection; scope is submitted specimens/common poisons, not all toxic agents.
Cleland mixed direct observation/comparison with opinion and requires transcription control.
Hicks is expert review testimony on poison groups and timing difficulties, not direct observation or poison identification.
Bennett examined body about 9:40 a.m. on 1 Dec and estimated death could have occurred up to eight hours earlier; not observed death time.
Hall identified bus-ticket route/issue context without identifying holder or boarding/alighting points.
Holdernesse had no independent recollection and supports record-based transport link only.
North produced luggage-ticket/suitcase evidence but did not issue ticket; actual issuer absent.
West gave railway timetable evidence but not proof deceased travelled by train.
Townsend gave Henley Beach ticket issue window but no purchaser memory or exact issue time.
Lawson directly supports cast/bust creation; assumption-based body/feet observations must be separated from description.
Authorized witness index contains original inquest witnesses only; later contributors are outside witness evidence for this chapter.
Modern identity/DNA/exhumation/genealogy statements are excluded from witness matrix unless in authorized witness/source-mapped registers.
Reliability criteria include opportunity, temporal proximity, consistency, corroboration, bias, memory, OCR/transcription quality, and evidentiary value.
Strong class includes Moss, Hall, Dwyer, Cowan, Durham, and Townsend for core observations/records with limits.
Moderate class includes WIT-0001, Lawson, Strapps, Neill, North, Leane, West, Brown, Cleland, and Bennett.
Weak/limited class includes Holdernesse for passenger identification and Hicks for event reconstruction scope.
No character judgment is assigned; impressions are not upgraded into fact; witness statements do not resolve hypotheses.
Table summarizes reliability class counts: 6 strong, 10 moderate, 2 weak/limited, 0 contradicted as false, 0 unknown.
Witness index table is a control table, not a credibility scorecard.
Complete witness index table lists WIT-0001 through WIT-0018 with role, page references, value, and limits.
Witness chronology maps each witness to period, related events, and principal limitation.
Witness-to-event map links witnesses to chronology IDs and evidence IDs with use limitations.
Principal limitations include time delay, OCR distortion, incomplete transcription, missing/restricted records, and observation/inference distinction; 55 pages are in transcription queue.
Memory limits are explicit for Holdernesse, Townsend, North, and evening beach witnesses.
Medical/scientific witness testimony must preserve scope limits: Cowan common-poison testing, Hicks possibilities, Bennett estimate, Cleland mixed observation/opinion.
Witness layer supports structured testimonial record, not completed explanation.
